---
title: "Data"
---

## Overview

The MC2 dataset represents Oceanus as a knowledge graph: entities are nodes, and relationships between them are edges. In this page, we break down the data, how we understood it, and the analytical decisions we made before working on the visualisations.

## The files

| File | Rows | Role in analysis |
|:--|:-:|:--|
| `mc2_journalist_links.csv` | 2,436 | **The journalist links file has the complete source of truth to identify any bias based on the organisations' claims.** Every link is a relationship between nodes in the journalist_nodes file, filling us with the full picture. |
| `mc2_jounalist_nodes.csv` | 740 | **The journalist nodes file contains every entity in the complete dataset**: meetings, people, topics, and travel destinations. All FILAH and TROUT nodes are fully in the file.|
| `mc2_FILAH_links.csv` | 765 | **FILAH's submitted evidence: a cherry-picked subset of the journalist links file.** Every record here exists in the journalist file; what differs is what FILAH chose to leave out to support their pro-tourism bias narrative. |
| `mc2_FILAH_nodes.csv` | 396 | **FILAH's node list, fully part of the journalist nodes file.** Node-level comparisons are handled through the links files and the journalist nodes as the single authoritative reference. |
| `mc2_TROUT_links.csv` | 378 | **TROUT's submitted evidence: a different cherry-picked subset of the journalist links file.** A smaller slice than FILAH's dataset, curated to suppress travel evidence and overrepresent fishing activity in support of their own counter-narrative. |
| `mc2_TROUT_nodes.csv` | 164 | **TROUT's node list, fully part of the journalist nodes file.** Notably smaller dataset, reflecting TROUT's narrower evidence pool. |
| `mc2_geo_nodes.csv` | 2,664 | **Physical locations in Oceanus with zone classification and coordinates.** Used to enrich travel links and determine whether COOTEFOO members' travel destinations skewed toward fishing or tourism zones. |
| `mc2_geo_edges.csv` | 3,420 | **The road network connecting locations across Oceanus.**|

: {tbl-colwidths="[20,10,65]"}

## The graph structure

Each links file contains edges between nodes. Every row is one connection. The critical column is `role`, which defines what kind of connection it is.

| Role | Count (journalist) | Source-Target | Analytical use |
|:--|:-:|:--|:--|
| `participant` | 218 | Discussion → Member or Organisation | **Primary analysis.** Only role that carries sentiment scores. |
| `about` | 200 | Discussion → Topic | Topic coverage analysis |
| `part_of` | 175 | Discussion → Meeting | Meeting structure |
| `plan` | 73 | Topic → Plan document | Output tracking |
| `travel` | 22 | Topic → Geographic location | Travel pattern analysis |
| `refers_to` | 43 | Document → Document | Cross-reference network |

::: {.callout-warning appearance="minimal"}
Only 218 of 2,436 rows are participant type. The remaining 2,218 rows carry no sentiment values: 1,705 have no role assigned at all, and the other 513 belong to structural roles like about, part_of, and plan. Every sentiment analysis in this project is filtered exclusively to participant links. Forgetting this filter produces meaningless averages across thousands of NULL sentiment values.
:::

Nodes contain: `type`, `id`, `name`, `role`, `label`,`short_topic`, `long_topic`, `lat`, `lon`, `zone`, `zone_detail`, `date`, `start`, `end`.

Node types present: `entity.person`, `entity.organization`, `meeting`, `discussion`, `plan`, `trip`, `place`, `topic`.

---

## The COOTEFOO board members

Six people sit on the COOTEFOO board. They appear as `target` values in `participant` links. Their presence or absence across the three datasets is itself evidence of bias.

| Member | Role | In FILAH | In TROUT | Journalist only |
|:--|:--|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Seal | Committee Chair | ✓ | ✓ | — |
| Simone Kat | Member | ✓ | — | — |
| Carol Limpet | Member | ✓ | — | — |
| Ed Helpsford | Vice Chair | — | ✓ | — |
| Teddy Goldstein | Treasurer | — | ✓ | — |
| Tante Titan | Member | — | — | ✓ |

::: {.callout-important appearance="minimal"}
FILAH recorded 3 members; TROUT recorded 3 members, but they share Seal in common. The two groups each omitted members that inconvenienced their narrative: FILAH dropped Ed Helpsford and Teddy Goldstein; TROUT dropped Simone Kat and Carol Limpet. Tante Titan, whose full record shows balanced, positive engagement across both industries, was excluded by both groups. 
:::

---

## The critical finding

Before building any visualisation, we analysed the data using the three links files using (`source + target + role`).

::: {.callout-important appearance="minimal"}
**Result: 100% of FILAH's 765 rows exist inside the journalist file. 100% of TROUT's 378 rows also exist inside the journalist file.**

The journalist file is a complete superset. FILAH and TROUT did not collect independent data, they each drew from the same pool of 2,436 records and selectively chose what to submit.
:::

| Segment | Count |
|:--|:-:|
| In FILAH only (not TROUT) | 586 |
| In TROUT only (not FILAH) | 199 |
| In both FILAH and TROUT | 179 |
| In Journalist only (neither group) | 1,472 |
| **Total unique journalist links** | **2,436** |

**1,472 records: 60% of the journalist's dataset were not presented by either activist group.**

---

## Understanding what "bias" means in this data

The data does not contain a direct "bias" column. Bias must be inferred from patterns. We identified three layers:

### Layer 1 — Sentiment bias

`participant` links carry a sentiment score from −1 (very negative) to +1 (very positive). If one group's data shows systematically different sentiment toward fishing vs tourism topics, that is evidence of bias in the data they chose to submit.

| Dataset | Fishing sentiment | Tourism sentiment |
|:--|:-:|:-:|
| FILAH | 0.334 | **0.679** |
| TROUT | **0.400** | 0.191 |
| Full Journalist | 0.449 | 0.531 |

FILAH's data makes the board look strongly pro-tourism. TROUT's data makes the board look strongly pro-fishing. The full dataset shows near-equal engagement with both. The gap between each group's view and reality is the quantified sampling bias.

### Layer 2 — Topic omission bias

Two topics are completely absent from one group's data:

::: {layout-ncol=2}
::: {}
**FILAH omitted:**  
`concert`: a Tourism topic  
Present in TROUT and Journalist  
Missing while accusing the board of **pro-tourism** bias
:::
::: {}
**TROUT omitted:**  
`seafood_festival`: a Fishing topic  
Present in FILAH and Journalist  
Missing while accusing the board of **pro-fishing** bias
:::
:::

These are not gaps from random sampling. Each group removed the topic that goes against their accusation.

### Layer 3 — Member exclusion bias

Beyond topics, the groups excluded entire people:

::: {layout-ncol=2}
::: {}
**FILAH excluded:**

1. Teddy Goldstein (Fishing=0.85, Tourism=−0.50)  
2. Ed Helpsford (Fishing=1.0)  
3. Tante Titan (balanced)
:::
::: {}

**TROUT excluded:**  
1. Simone Kat (Tourism=0.91)  
2. Carol Limpet  
3. Tante Titan (balanced)
:::
:::

The pattern is consistent: each group excluded members whose records would have weakened their narrative and included those whose records strengthened it.

<table class="table">
<thead>
<tr>
<th style="text-align:left;">FILAH excluded</th>
<th style="text-align:left;">TROUT excluded</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><b>Teddy Goldstein <br>(Fishing=0.786, Tourism=−0.500)</b>
<br>Strong pro-fishing, anti-tourism record would have undermined FILAH's narrative</td>
<td><b>Simone Kat <br>(Fishing=0.379, Tourism=0.908)</b>
<br>Strongly pro-tourism record would have undermined TROUT's narrative</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><b>Ed Helpsford <br>(Fishing=1.0, Tourism=0.714)</b> 
<br>Uniformly positive across both industries, not a useful villain</td>
<td><b>Carol Limpet <br>(Fishing=0.750, Tourism=0.682)</b>
<br>Balanced across both industries, not useful for TROUT's case either</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td colspan="2" class="align-middle" style="text-align: center;"><b>Tante Titan <br>(Fishing=0.750, Tourism=0.471)</b>
<br>Both excluded: Positive and balanced across both industries; directly contradicts both groups' bias claims</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## How we structured the data in Tableau

The journalist links file became the single base table. FILAH and TROUT links were left-joined onto it using a two-field composite key.

```
Base: mc2_journalist_links.csv (2,436 rows)
  └─ LEFT JOIN mc2_FILAH_links.csv  ON source + target 
  └─ LEFT JOIN mc2_TROUT_links.csv  ON source + target 
```

After the join, rows where FILAH's `source` column is NULL were excluded by FILAH. Rows where TROUT's `source` column is NULL were excluded by TROUT. Two boolean calculated fields (`In FILAH`, `In TROUT`) capture this. A parameter (`Dataset View`) switches the filter between FILAH, TROUT, and Full Journalist perspectives without requiring separate data sources.

The journalist nodes file was added twice as related tables — once for target node attributes, once for source node attributes. The geo nodes file was added as a third relationship, keyed on `target = id`, resolving only for travel links where the target is a geographic node ID.

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Why not union?** Unioning all three links files would duplicate the 765 FILAH rows and 378 TROUT rows that already exist in the journalist file, inflating all counts by up to 3×. The join approach preserves exactly 2,436 rows. 
:::

---

## Classification of Topics 

<table class="table">
<thead>
<tr>
<th style="text-align:left;">Industry</th>
<th style="text-align:left;">Topic</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td rowspan="6" class="align-middle">Fishing</td>
<td><code>fish_vacuum</code></td>
</tr>
<tr><td><code>deep_fishing_dock</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>new_crane_lomark</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>low_volume_crane</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>seafood_festival</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>affordable_housing</code></td></tr>
<tr>
<td rowspan="5" class="align-middle">Tourism</td>
<td><code>expanding_tourist_wharf</code></td>
</tr>
<tr><td><code>marine_life_deck</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>waterfront_market</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>heritage_walking_tour</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>concert</code></td></tr>
<tr>
<td rowspan="4" class="align-middle">Other</td>
<td><code>statue_john_smoth</code></td>
</tr>
<tr><td><code>renaming_park_himark</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>name_harbor_area</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>name_inspection_office</code></td></tr>
</tbody>
</table>

::: {.callout-caution appearance="minimal"}
Topic classification was not provided in the data: we assigned each topic to an industry category based on the short-form topic description in the nodes file. The topics were grouped based on our assumption of fishing and tourism activites. 
:::
